title: Children separated at a public shelter
summary: A caregiver procedure for reducing panic and improving reunification when children are separated in a school, ferry terminal, gym, camp, or emergency shelter.
content:
A caregiver procedure for reducing panic and improving reunification when children are separated in a school, ferry terminal, gym, camp, or emergency shelter.

This card keeps children separated at a public shelter practical: define the owner, protect the vulnerable point, make the result visible, and keep one review time. It is designed for phone use under stress, not for perfect paperwork.

Core sequence: 1) Teach children one safe adult name, one meeting point, and one phrase to say before the event if possible. 2) When separation happens, stay at the last agreed point unless danger forces movement. 3) Give officials the child’s name, age, clothing, last seen location, language, medical needs, and trusted pickup names. 4) Do not publish the child’s full details publicly; use official reunification channels first.

Stop point: Do not use children separated at a public shelter to justify entering a place that smells unsafe, sounds unstable, is electrically wet, or has been closed by authorities.
materials: child reunification card reserved for Children separated at a public shelter
dated note for the owner of Children separated at a public shelter
separate clean storage point for Children · Shelter separation
visible stop mark for Children separated at a public shelter
steps: Teach children one safe adult name, one meeting point, and one phrase to say before the event if possible.
When separation happens, stay at the last agreed point unless danger forces movement.
Give officials the child’s name, age, clothing, last seen location, language, medical needs, and trusted pickup names.
Do not publish the child’s full details publicly; use official reunification channels first.
warnings: Do not treat children separated at a public shelter as routine when official instructions, injury, fire, contaminated water, or structural danger change the situation.
Do not keep private names, medicine details, or locations visible longer than children separated at a public shelter genuinely requires.
common_mistakes: Beginning children separated at a public shelter without a named owner makes good supplies and correct advice hard to use.
Letting old notes guide children separated at a public shelter after conditions change can send people toward yesterday’s hazard.
alternatives: If children separated at a public shelter cannot be completed, finish only the safest visible marker and write what still needs checking.
If the normal tool is missing, switch to paper, spoken confirmation, and a simpler one-person handover for children separated at a public shelter.
failure_signs: People disagree about the current status of children separated at a public shelter because the last change was not dated.
The person most affected by children separated at a public shelter cannot access the result, read the notice, or ask for help.
when_not_to_use: Do not use children separated at a public shelter to justify entering a place that smells unsafe, sounds unstable, is electrically wet, or has been closed by authorities.
Do not continue children separated at a public shelter if it prevents a 112 call or hides a serious health warning.
short_term: Complete the first visible result for children separated at a public shelter and confirm the next review time before moving to a lower priority task.
long_term: After real use, improve children separated at a public shelter by removing steps nobody understood and adding the local contact or storage point that was missing.
if_method_fails: If children separated at a public shelter fails, stop the routine, protect the person or area at highest risk, and restart with one owner and one written action.
environment_notes: Adapt children separated at a public shelter for apartments, villages, islands, camps, poor lighting, heat, rain, crowds, low battery, and limited mobility.
